text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

Hello and welcome back toi’s live coverage of the2024 Olympic Games.

Keely Hodgkinson is the new Olympic champion in the women’s 800m, the 22-year-old Brit winning Team GB’s second gold medal of the day after Emma Finucane, Sophie Capewell and Katy Marchant set their third women’s team sprint world record in a matter of hours to clinch victory in the opening track cycling event of Paris 2024.

That followed back-to-back medals in thekayak crossfinals as Kimberley Woods won her second bronze of these Games in the women’s final moments before Joe Clarke grabbed a silver medal in the men’s bracket.

GB’s first medal of the day – a bronze – came after a dramatic photo-finish showed the British team were pipped into third place in the mixed triathlon relay this morning.

Elsewhere, Simone Biles won her 11th total Olympic medal but it was a silver as the American was beaten in the floor exercise final by Brazil’s Rebeca Andrade to close out the artistic gymnastics events.

Plus, we are expecting updates onboxing’s gender rowandswimming’s doping row.

This live blog has now closed.

So there you have it.

Keely Hodgkinson isthe women’s Olympic 800m champion.

Never in doubt!

It was also a great night for Team GB’s women sprint trio.

See you tomorrow morning for more entertainment.

Culture Secretary Lisa Nandy has called Olympic 800m champion Keely Hodgkinson “an incredible role model”.

The Cabinet minister wrote on X: “The whole country is cheering Keely right now but nowhere more so than at Leigh Harriers.” Leigh Harriers and Athletic Club is in Lancashire, near Ms Nandy’s Wigan constituency.

She added: “The pride of our borough and our country. What an incredible role model she is.”

STADE DE FRANCE– Armand Duplantis has done it! A new pole vault world record for the Swede, who already held it of course, and the crowd erupts for him.

STADE DE FRANCE– Hodgkinson is now running down the back straight high-fiving half of Paris.

She’s now found two lads in Union Jack bucket hats with a sign saying “Well done Keely, can I have a selfie?” and is obliging, naturally.

At the pace she is going, I believe this is part warmdown, part victory lap.

STADE DE FRANCE– Crikey, life moves fast on this athletics track.

The 800m runners hadn’t even completed a fraction of their victory lap before Duplantis fever hit, as the Swedish pole vaulter – who has already secured his gold medal – breaks the Olympic pole vault record by jumping 6.10m.

Dupantis is going for a world record now. Another one.

The current world record, which he set in April this year, stands at 6.24m.

STADE DE FRANCE–  Well, well, well. Keely Hodgkinson led from the gun to win GB’s first athletics gold medal of these Olympics. She looks overwhelmed at the end of that race.

Gold for Keely Hodgkinson in the women’s 800m final!

The 22-year-old Brit goes one step further up the podium than she managed in Tokyo three years ago, when she took silver and broke the British record set by Kelly Holmes in 1995.

Her winning mark of 1:56.72 in this race is not a best time for Hodgkinson, but she won’t care too much about that – she’s just won an Olympic gold medal.

STADE DE FRANCE–  Armand Duplantis is going for an Olympic record of 6.10m. He has already clinched the gold medal, this is the encore.

He’ll have to wait until after the 800m though – that’s just started, with GB’s Keely Hodgkinson going for gold.

Hang on a minute…

It appears Kenya’s Faith Kipyegon, who just crossed the finish line second in that women’s 5,000m final, has been disqualified – reportedly for a mid-race collision (‘obstructing’ or ‘jostling’, per a violation of rule 17.1.2, our Matt Butler tells me) with Ethiopia’s Gudaf Tsegay.

That means disregard our last few results posts, as Sifan Hassan of the Netherlands sees her medal upgraded from bronze to silver while Italy’s Nadia Battocletti is the new Olympic bronze medallist.

Kipyegon’s compatriot, Beatrice Chebet, is still the gold medallist with her time of 14:28.56.

STADE DE FRANCE– That was an incredible battle between [Beatrice] Chebet and [Faith] Kipyegon for gold.

The sprint finish started at the beginning of the back straight.

STADE DE FRANCE– Hassan is beaten!

Beatrice Chebet and Faith Kipyegon, both of Kenya, kicked together down the back straight and the Netherlands’ Sifan Hassan was never able to strike back.

Chebet takes gold ahead of team-mate and world champion Kipyegon, while Hassan has to settle for bronze.

STADE DE FRANCE– Meanwhile at the pole vault, it is a three-way battle at 6m for the medals, between Duplantis, Kendricks and Emmanouil.

The Olympic record is 6.03m. I think it will be broken here.

STADE DE FRANCE– Matt makes a fair point about the conditions, I have got a bead on up here. And so has Grovdal, who is cooked. It’s now the Ethopian pair of Tsegay and Taye at the front.

The Kenyan team are three abreast behind them, and then Hassan ready to pounce, five or six metres off the lead. One lap to go, Hassan still waiting, but the Kenyans are making a move as a three…

STADE DE FRANCE– I think Karoline Grovdal heard me [see 8.17pm post] and has gone to the front to sort it out. She ran at London 2012 by the way, and is the European half-marathon champion, so a sprint finish is no good to her, hence the move.

I’m all for it.

Matt Butler points out that it is 27 degrees and humid for this 5,000m race.

STADE DE FRANCE– Oh my word, I was concentrating so much on the pole vault and the 200m that it had slipped my mind that Dutch phenomenon Sifan Hassan is running the 5,000m for her first of aZatopek-esque three possible gold medals, in the 5,000m, 10,000m and marathon.

They’ve just set off now.

Hassan has gone straight to the back and they’ve gone through the first 400m in a ‘pedestrian’ 76 seconds, as my colleague James Gray has called it.

It’s a chess match at 180 heartbeats a minute.

STADE DE FRANCE– It’s going to be tough to bust a medal out of this women’s 200m contest tomorrow. Some high-end sprinters eating up the metres.

Here’s your top eight qualifiers and their semi-final times, with Daryll Neita the faster of the two British women heading into the final:

GB’s Bianca Williams finished 10th overall with a season best time of 22.58.

STADE DE FRANCE– Daryll Neita said after the London Diamond League meeting that when racing against her good friend Dina Asher Smith, she wants to “destroy” her.

Well, she will get the chance tomorrow night in the 200m final.

Neita makes it through her semi-final heat in second, behind the USA’s Brittany Brown, to qualify for the final.

STADE DE FRANCE– Pole vault must be rife with mind games.

Sam Kendricks of the USA, Earnest John of the Philippines and Emmanouil Karalis of Greece share the lead having cleared 5.90m, but they have all jumped five times.

Armand Duplantis has only jumped twice and skipped the 5.90 height (it is a full 34cm shy of his world record).

The Swede has spent most of the time sitting around chatting to the other jumpers. I would love to know the line of conversation. “So, that looked really high, were you OK on that one?”

STADE DE FRANCE– Dina Asher-Smith runs a 22.31 and is through to the final in second behind one of the hot 200m favourites Gabrielle Thomas of the USA, who was a cut above at 21.86 seconds.

A fast heat in that second semi-final ends Bianca Williams’s hopes of progressing to the final as a fastest loser, though.

Daryll Neita, whofinished fourth in the 100m at the weekend, still to come in this for GB.

STADE DE FRANCE– Women’s 100m champ Julien Alfred chalks up another win in the first heat of the women’s 200m semi-finals.

GB’s Bianca Williams finished fourth and must wait to see if her season best 22.58 gets her through to the final as one of the two fastest heat losers.

Dina Asher-Smith and Daryll Neita are representing Britain in the second and third semi-finals, respectively.

Impartial news + intelligent debate

title: Hodgkinson wins Olympic 800m gold after GB women set team sprint world record
author: Alex Dakers, Evan Bartlett, Tom Ward
description: Follow i's live blog for updates from the Olympic Games in Paris
